danger_signals.visual_form: Stage 4 boss character design (unmasked female with long hair in JP version, masked male with short hair in NA version)
danger_signals.meaning: Visual distinction indicates threat/enemy status; character transformation signals danger escalation
Masked/male form conveys threat more directly than unmasked/feminine form; visual coding of antagonism

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Bosses and stage enemies contrast with protagonist

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Sonia (female character), Captain Choyear (military/authority figure implied by rank)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Character selection indicates allied playable forces

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Regional political/cultural context (Japan vs North America)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Boss character redesign: unmasked female → masked male; protagonist dialogue change: 'Japanese line' → 'I FIGHT FOR FREEDOM!'
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Narrative framing shifts from unspecified Japanese context to explicit Western freedom-fighting narrative; same mechanical threat presented through different visual/textual identity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Boss character introduction at Stage 4 signals mid-to-late game threat escalation

environmental_storytelling.example: Boss character regional variant communicates cultural expectations: Japanese version presents unmasked female antagonist; Western version presents masked male, aligning with localization cultural norms and threat perception
Protagonist's regional speech variation ('I FIGHT FOR FREEDOM!' in NA) establishes ideological stance and positions player motivation within specific geopolitical framework

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Threat entity that must be defeated; visual redesign between regions indicates mechanical function remains constant while narrative threat framing changes

entity_behavioral_tells.entity: Playable characters (Sonic Blast Man, Sonia, Captain Choyear)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Player-controlled agents; character roster expansion signals multiplayer faction diversity and cooperative/competitive modes

notes: Sonic Blast Man II communicates world-state primarily through character design decisions and localization choices rather than environmental visual systems. The most significant manifestation patterns are found in regional differences: the Stage 4 boss redesign and protagonist dialogue shifts establish ideological/political framing for the same gameplay content. The game presents a beat 'em up paradigm where faction identity is largely character-based rather than environmentally or symbolically embedded. The addition of diverse playable characters (Sonia, Captain Choyear) suggests faction/team multiplicity without explicit visual coding beyond character sprite distinction. Limited environmental storytelling techniques are documented; world-state communication relies on character presentation and narrative framing rather than dynamic visual manifestation systems.
